narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest 10th decile based on your grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success using patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 72.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers (far below the typical 5th or 6th decile), signaling to the model a notable dip in academic momentum that often correlates with bar struggles. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% (again undercutting the average), reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively—nearly matching the dataset average of 3.21—and it worked in your favor, nudging the model's outlook slightly toward bar passage success. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on their first try, your LSAT score of 31 trails the average of 36.7, but it wasn't among the top influences here; full-time status, common for 93% of students, was neutral. These early-grade deciles carried the heaviest weight because they reflect sustained performance trends that the model sees as key predictors for Hispanic and other underrepresented students navigating rigorous legal training. While your solid GPA offers a bright spot, the model prioritized those lower deciles as red flags based on historical data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, seeking academic coaching to improve your school rankings in future terms, or exploring part-time programs if that fits your goals better—many students in similar situations have strengthened their profiles this way and succeeded on appeal or at other schools. You're clearly driven, and with targeted improvements, you can shift this narrative in your favor.
